title: First-principles simulation of shocked H-He mixture along the principal Hugoniot
authors: Ammar A. Ellaboudy, Valentin V. Karasiev, S. X. Hu
abstract: Recent laser-shock experiments on an H--He mixture containing 11~$\%$ helium (atomic fraction) have suggested the presence of an immiscibility region inside Jupiter. Reflectivity measurements were used as the primary diagnostic of H--He demixing, with discontinuities in the optical reflectivity proposed as a signature of phase separation under conditions relevant to Jupiter's interior. Here, we investigate shock-compressed H--He using \textit{ab initio} molecular dynamics simulations with optical properties evaluated within the Kubo--Greenwood formalism. The equation of state and ionic configurations were obtained using the thermal Tr$^2$SCANL meta-GGA exchange--correlation (XC) functional, while optical properties were computed using the recently developed RS-KDT0 range-separated thermal hybrid XC, which provides state-of-the-art accuracy for band-gap predictions in the warm dense matter regime. The calculated reflectivity shows overall good agreement with experimental measurements; however, no discontinuity is observed at elevated temperatures. Moreover, the reflectivity predictions for the mixed system are consistent with the experimental measurements in the temperature range where the mixture is inferred to be demixed. These results suggest that reflectivity alone may not provide a unique or sensitive diagnostic of H-He demixing at low helium concentrations under these conditions.
summary: This paper uses advanced first-principles simulations to study the optical properties of shock-compressed hydrogen-helium mixtures. It concludes that changes in optical reflectivity may not be a definitive signature of phase separation at low helium concentrations, challenging previous experimental interpretations of H-He demixing.
main_problem: Investigating whether optical reflectivity discontinuities can serve as a reliable diagnostic for H-He immiscibility (phase separation) under shock-compressed conditions relevant to Jupiter's interior.
main_result: The simulations show no reflectivity discontinuity at elevated temperatures and find that reflectivity predictions for a mixed system are consistent with experimental data in the demixing regime, suggesting reflectivity alone is not a sensitive diagnostic for low helium concentrations.
method: Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations using the thermal Tr2SCANL meta-GGA functional for the equation of state and the RS-KDT0 range-separated thermal hybrid XC functional for optical properties via the Kubo-Greenwood formalism.
model_or_system: A shock-compressed mixture of Hydrogen and Helium containing 11% helium (atomic fraction) under warm dense matter conditions.
key_observables: Optical reflectivity, equation of state, and ionic configurations.
important_parameters: 11% helium atomic fraction, shock-compressed regime, warm dense matter regime.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the thermal Tr2SCANL and RS-KDT0 exchange-correlation functionals for predicting properties in the warm dense matter regime.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of H-He demixing in Jupiter, describes the ab initio molecular dynamics and Kubo-Greenwood methodology, presents the calculated reflectivity and equation of state, and concludes by comparing results to experimental laser-shock data to evaluate the diagnostic utility of reflectivity.
